result: Dusk is a secret door. I step through it every evening without meaning to, the way one drifts into a memory. The sky becomes a bowl of spilled ink, violet and rose bleeding outward, and suddenly the ordinary yard is a foreign coastline. Crickets rehearse their small metallic hymns; somewhere a neighbor’s sprinkler ticks like a lazy metronome. Time loosens its collar. I notice the cherry tree has been writing letters to the sidewalk again—dark bruise-colored syllables that will be meaningless by morning. I collect one leaf, still warm from the sun’s last exhale, and tuck it into my pocket like a forged passport. Inside, the kitchen light is brutal, fluorescent, a courtroom interrogation. Out here, the dark is generous. It gives every shadow the benefit of the doubt. I think of people I’ve lost: how they keep misplacing their faces in my dreams, showing up with the wrong eyes, borrowed voices. The moon climbs the roof, a polite burglar. I do not call the police. Instead I offer it coffee, stale from the morning, and ask if it ever gets tired of being everybody’s abandoned coin. It says nothing, but the tide of light on the shingles answers: everything is currency, even silence, even the hush between heartbeats when you realize you are still here, still renting this moment, still capable of wonder. I close the door, leave the leaf on the counter. By dawn it will have crumbled into a small green earthquake. I will sweep it up, start the kettle, pretend the world is brand new.

The sky becomes a bowl of spilled ink, violet and rose bleeding outward, and suddenly the ordinary yard is a foreign coastline. Crickets rehearse their small metallic hymns; somewhere a neighbor’s sprinkler ticks like a lazy metronome. Time loosens its collar. I notice the cherry tree has been writing letters to the sidewalk again—dark bruise-colored syllables that will be meaningless by morning. I collect one leaf, still warm from the sun’s last exhale, and tuck it into my pocket like a forged passport. Inside, the kitchen light is brutal, fluorescent, a courtroom interrogation. Out here, the dark is generous. It gives every shadow the benefit of the doubt. I think of people I’ve lost: how they keep misplacing their faces in my dreams, showing up with the wrong eyes, borrowed voices. The moon climbs the roof, a polite burglar. I do not call the police. Instead I offer it coffee, stale from the morning, and ask if it ever gets tired of being everybody’s abandoned coin. It says nothing, but the tide of light on the shingles answers: everything is currency, even silence, even the hush between heartbeats when you realize you are still here, still renting this moment, still capable of wonder. I close the door, leave the leaf on the counter. By dawn it will have crumbled into a small green earthquake. I will sweep it up, start the kettle, pretend the world is brand new.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
